Box shadows are one of those CSS properties that look simple until you are actually tuning them. A single box-shadow declaration has five values, and most real-world designs stack multiple layers to create depth, glows, or lifted-card effects.
The result is a lot of trial and error. You write a value, inspect in DevTools, adjust the blur, change the spread, repeat. The code eventually looks right, but the process burns more time than it should.
A visual generator changes that workflow.
What BoxShadowGen Solves
At https://boxshadowgen.com, you can build single or multi-layer box shadows visually with a live preview and copy production-ready CSS output when you are satisfied.
Useful when you need to:

• Create realistic depth effects for cards and panels

• Build glows, neumorphic effects, or inner shadows

• Stack multiple shadow layers with independent control over each

• Match a design reference without guessing at values

• Generate consistent shadow tokens for a design system
Why Box Shadows Are Harder Than They Look
The syntax is deceptively compact:
box-shadow: offsetX offsetY blurRadius spreadRadius color;But real designs often combine:

• Outer and inner (inset) shadows on the same element

• Multiple layers at different opacities to simulate ambient and key lighting

• Color-tinted shadows for neon or glass effects

• Zero-blur spread shadows for sharp outlines
Getting that combination right manually takes iteration. Visual tooling reduces the cost of that iteration.
Faster Iteration, Production-Ready Output
A good shadow generator should do two things:

• Let you iterate fast with immediate visual feedback

• Output CSS you can paste directly into your codebase
BoxShadowGen focuses on both. Per-layer controls let you tune offset, blur, spread, color, opacity, and inset independently. The live preview shows the result on a neutral background so you can judge depth accurately.
Typical Workflow

• Open a preset close to your target effect

• Tune offset values to match your light source direction

• Adjust blur and spread for the desired softness

• Add a second layer for ambient shadow if needed

• Toggle inset for inner shadow effects

• Copy the CSS and paste into your project
This workflow is faster than building from scratch in DevTools and produces cleaner multi-layer output than manual editing.
When to Use a Generator vs Hand-Writing
Use a generator when:

• You are matching a design comp

• You are building a multi-layer effect

• You want to explore shadow styles quickly

• You need consistent tokens across multiple components
Hand-write from scratch when:

• The shadow is a single-layer utility with known values

• You are updating an existing token, not creating a new one
For anything more than a simple single-layer shadow, starting visual and refining the output saves time.
